name: Julius Watkins
abstract: Julius Watkins (October 10, 1921 – April 4, 1977) was an American jazz musician who played French horn. Described by AllMusic as "virtually the father of the jazz French horn", Watkins won the Down Beat critics poll in 1960 and 1961 for Miscellaneous Instrument.

concerts.label: Bel Canto Foundation Jazz Concert to Benefit Musicians
Miles Davis with Gil Evans and Orchestra
New York Jazz Repertory Company: Salute to Thelonious Monk

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: And Julius Watkins on trombone – uh, Jimmy Cleveland on trombone and Julius Watkins on french horn. That’s right. That was my first date as a leader.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: It was wonderful working with Julius Watkins, he was a beautiful man and a wonderful musician.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ernie Fields
about_quotes.evidence: So as soon as I had a trumpet opening, I got Julius and Julius spent quite a while in the band

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Varner
about_quotes.evidence: I just stuck with it and then when I found out from a next door neighbor about Julius Watkins that helped me realize well then – Julius Watkins who actually went to high school I think with Sheila Jordan
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Varner interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, August 13, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: features the man himself with Phil Urso, tenor, Julius Watkins, french horn, Walter Bishop, piano, Charles Mingus, bass and Percy Brice, drums.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Wormworth
about_quotes.evidence: And we had worked together before that with Les Jazz Modes, a quintet that Charlie Rouse and Julius Watkins co-led.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Wormworth interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002
